# Client Pricing Proposals

Pricing web development services for the Paraguayan market. Covers tier structure, domain/hosting scenarios, setup fees, and how to present to clients.

## Core Pricing (USER CONFIRMED — Fixed Setup Across All Tiers)

IMPORTANT: The user confirmed a FIXED setup fee of 450,000 Gs for ALL plans. Do NOT present variable per-tier setup as an option unless asked. This is settled.

### SOLO HOSTING
- Setup: 450,000 Gs (fixed)
- Monthly: 97,000 Gs
- Annual: 970,000 Gs (saves 194,000 Gs vs monthly)
- Client already has a website, just needs hosting
- SSL + backups + soporte técnico incluidos

### PLAN BÁSICO
- Setup: 450,000 Gs (fixed)
- Monthly: 187,000 Gs
- Annual: 1,935,000 Gs (saves 290,000 Gs vs monthly)
- 5 pages (Inicio, Nosotros, Servicios, Contacto, Galería)
- WhatsApp integration, contact form, 10 photos
- Basic SEO, map, mobile responsive

### PLAN PROFESIONAL
- Setup: 450,000 Gs (fixed)
- Monthly: 381,000 Gs
- Annual: 3,870,000 Gs (saves 580,000 Gs vs monthly)
- 10 pages, team profiles, online booking, blog (5 posts/mo)
- 30 photos, advanced SEO

### PLAN EMPRESA
- Setup: 450,000 Gs (fixed)
- Monthly: 639,000 Gs
- Annual: 6,450,000 Gs (saves 1,290,000 Gs vs monthly)
- Unlimited pages, e-commerce, multiple locations
- Unlimited photos, full blog, priority support

### Annual Plan Bonus
If client pays the full year upfront, setup is FREE. The annual price already has ~2 months effectively free built in.

## Setup Fee Options (Historical — Only Present If User Asks)

The user has confirmed a fixed 450K Gs setup. Do NOT offer alternatives unless the user explicitly revisits this decision.

- **Fixed setup (current):** 450,000 Gs across all tiers. Rationale: onboarding work (relevamiento, diseño, contenido, dominio, SSL, WhatsApp, pruebas, entrega) is the same regardless of plan.
- **Zero setup (contract anchor):** formerly discussed. Setup 0 Gs with minimum 6-month contract. The "48-hour hook" absorbs setup as acquisition cost.

## Domain / Hosting Scenarios

### A — Client Already Has a Domain
Client keeps their domain (e.g., sunstein.com.ar). We point DNS to our infrastructure. No extra domain cost.

### B — We Use Paragu-IA Subdomain
Client gets a free subdomain (sunstein.paragu-ai.com). No domain purchase needed. Lowest friction, fastest setup.

### C — We Buy the Domain
We register and pay for the domain (.com or .com.py). Add ~97,000 Gs (15 USD) one-time to the setup fee.

### D — Hosting Only
Client already has a fully built website. We just host it on our Docker Swarm infrastructure. Setup: 0 Gs, Monthly: 97,000 Gs.

### E — Redesign + Migration
Client has a website but it's outdated or poorly built. We redesign from scratch and host. Setup: from 645,000 Gs (depends on page count). Monthly: per chosen plan.

### F — Annual Plan (Prepaid)
Setup free on all plans if client pays full year upfront.
- Básico: 2,064,000 Gs/yr (saves 322,000 vs monthly)
- Profesional: 4,129,000 Gs/yr
- Empresa: 6,967,000 Gs/yr

### G — Maintenance Only
For existing clients who just want to keep their site alive. Security updates, backups, SSL, minor support.
Monthly: 65,000 Gs (10 USD)

### H — Web + SEO + Google Ads Combo
Any plan + advanced local SEO + Google Ads setup and management.
- Setup: plan cost + 322,000 Gs
- Monthly: plan cost + 129,000 Gs

## Pricing for Creative Portfolio Sites (Artist/Photographer/Tattoo)

This is a **distinct pricing class** — single-artist portfolio sites with no e-commerce, no CMS, no multi-page complexity. They are simpler to build and maintain than B2C stores or B2B lead gen sites.

**Model:** Free setup, monthly-only billing.

**Pricing bands:**
- $100/mo — bare minimum, works if you're acquiring the client at a loss
- $150/mo — good "lanzamiento" price, can bump to $200 on renewal
- $200/mo — fair price for Docker Swarm hosting + HTTPS + galería + WhatsApp + backups + maintenance + content updates
- Do NOT go below $100/mo — the infrastructure cost (VPS, SSL, monitoring, Docker) plus maintenance time doesn't justify less

**Pitch angle for the client:**
"No pagás por la página, pagás porque la página funciona sola — galería actualizada, SSL, backups, WhatsApp funcionando, sin mantener nada. Cada vez que hacés un trabajo nuevo, mandás la foto y lo publicamos."

**Why the standard tiered model doesn't apply:** Creative portfolios are visually-driven, single-operator, don't need 10 pages, don't need blog, don't need booking. The "Profesional" tier ($381K Gs/mo) is overkill. The hosting-only tier ($97K Gs/mo ≈ $15 USD) is too little for the ongoing service. This is a middle ground.

**Origin:** Discussed during the Jota Ink session (May 2026). Ivan suggested $100-200/mo range. Recommendation settled at $150-200/mo.

The user landed on: **3 months free for the first e-commerce client (1000+ products).**

Pitch for the first client:
- Setup: 0 Gs
- 3 months completely free
- Month 4 onward: full Empresa price (639,000 Gs/mo)
- In exchange: testimonial, 30-second video, authorization as case study, referrals

Rationale: "Tu tienda online con 1000 productos funcionando sin pagar nada hasta el mes 4." The user wants to use this to build reputation, get testimonials, and generate referrals from day one.

## Sales Pitch Scripts

Use `/root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/GUION_VENTAS_PARAGUAI.md` for the complete sales script:
- Approach message
- Price explanation by plan
- Common objections and responses
- First-client e-commerce close (zero setup + 3 months free)
- Post-sale follow-up cadence

## Annual Plan Discount Structure

Annual pricing = monthly price × 12, with ~2 months effectively free:
- Básico: 1,935,000 Gs/yr (vs 2,244,000 Gs if monthly)
- Profesional: 3,870,000 Gs/yr
- Empresa: 6,450,000 Gs/yr
- Solo Hosting: 970,000 Gs/yr
- Setup is FREE if annual is paid upfront.

## What Setup Fee Covers

This is the #1 client question. Be prepared to explain concretely:

1. Relevamiento inicial — entender el negocio, servicios, colores/logo, fotos
2. Creación de contenido — textos de cada página si el cliente no los tiene
3. Diseño y maquetación — estructura visual personalizada, no plantilla genérica
4. Carga de fotos, logo, mapa — imágenes del cliente + Google Maps configurado
5. Integración WhatsApp API — botón configurado con su número real
6. Dominio y DNS — configurar DNS, crear subdominio, o registrar dominio nuevo
7. SSL / HTTPS — certificado de seguridad
8. SEO inicial — título, descripción, palabras clave
9. Pruebas — revisar en celular y PC, formularios, WhatsApp
10. Entrega y capacitación — mostrar al cliente cómo funciona y cómo pedir cambios

The 48-hour delivery is real — the technology (Next.js + Docker Swarm + automation) enables fast deploy. The setup fee covers the human personalization work, not server time.

## Iterative Pricing Workflow (USER PATTERN)

When the user asks for pricing or proposals (not presenting TO a client — working WITH Ivan/Kiki):

1. **Don't dump all options.** Ask what scenario/type first, or offer ONE option and wait for feedback.
2. **Present one option at a time.** Example: offer fixed setup first. User reacts → propose alternative. Not "Option A: fixed, Option B: zero, Option C: per-tier" all at once.
3. **Let the user choose direction.** They will say "I like this one" or "what if we do X instead." Follow their lead.
4. **Confirm before moving forward.** When they pick, explicitly confirm: "¿Entonces vamos con setup fijo de 450K para todos los planes?" Don't assume.
5. **Only present historical alternatives if user asks.** The skill stores past decisions so you don't re-litigate, but if user asks "what about zero setup?" present it as one option to compare.

This pattern emerged from a May 2026 session where Ivan was presented 3 setup fee options at once and had to work through them iteratively. The lesson: pricing is co-created, not delivered.

## Output Format Rules

IMPORTANT — based on user feedback:

- Do NOT present pricing in tables/markdown tables. Tables are unreadable on WhatsApp.
- Use plain text lists with clear labels:
  ```
  PLAN BÁSICO — 322,000 Gs setup + 187,000 Gs/mes
  5 páginas + WhatsApp + formulario + galería. Ideal para emprendedores.
  ```
- Start with the simplest summary first, then drill into details only on request.
- Always include Guarani prices by default (this is the Paraguayan market).
- Include USD in parentheses for reference.
- Offer "what each tier includes" in sentence form, not bullet-point columns.
- When providing multiple scenarios, label them clearly as A, B, C etc.

## Common Objections & Responses

**"Why is there a setup fee if you deliver in 48 hours?"**
The 48 hours is the technical delivery time. The setup covers the personalization work — understanding your business, writing your content, designing your pages, configuring your domain. The server deployment itself takes minutes.

**"Can I just pay monthly and skip the setup?"**
The setup covers work that only happens once. It's separate from the monthly hosting and maintenance.

**"What if I don't like it?"**
(If applicable) We set clear expectations during relevamiento so you know exactly what you're getting. Changes during build are covered. Post-launch changes go through monthly maintenance.

**"The free competitor offers everything for free"**
Our free plan exists too (time-limited, 6 months or X revenue). The paid plans add professional features: your own domain, no branding, blog, online booking, e-commerce, priority support.

## Pitfalls

- **Don't use markdown tables** for WhatsApp responses — they won't render.
- **Always include Guarani prices** — this is the local market, USD-only confuses clients.
- **Don't offer too many options at once** — lead with the simplest structure, expand only on request.
- **The setup fee explanation is critical** — clients hear "48 hours" and think the setup should be free. Preempt this objection.
- **Confirm the setup fee strategy** (variable, fixed, or zero) with the user before building a proposal — they have strong opinions here.
- **Don't bury the 48-hour value proposition** — it's the hook. When presenting zero-setup options, lead with it.
- **Keep plan descriptions short** — 1 sentence per plan, not a feature list.
- **For PYG conversion**: use ~6,450 Gs/USD (check current rate if precise).
- **Round Guarani prices to clean numbers** — 322,000 not 322,500. Clients read clean numbers easier.
- **Single-page-single-client** — these are individual client proposals, not a SaaS pricing page. Tailor to the prospect's business type.
- **Check if the repo is archived before pushing.** If push fails with 403, check repo status with `gh repo view --json isArchived` and unarchive with `gh repo unarchive` before pushing.

## Verification

- [ ] Guarani + USD prices included
- [ ] Setup fee structure confirmed with user (variable/fixed/zero)
- [ ] Domain scenario confirmed
- [ ] No markdown tables used in output
- [ ] 48-hour hook present where applicable
- [ ] Plan descriptions in sentence form, not feature bullet columns
- [ ] Objection preempted if setup fee is charged

## References

- See `references/` for session-specific proposal drafts and pricing iterations
- For building named-prospect sales pitches, see the `client-sales-pitch` skill (management category)
- The source pricing documents live in `/root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/`
  - `PRICING_STRATEGY.md` — full pricing framework (USD)
  - `FINANCIAL_MODEL.md` — revenue projections and unit economics
  - `PRICING_DEEP_RESEARCH.md` — research and psychology behind the model

", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "--- name: multi-source-research description: > Layer multiple data sources (free → API → MCP → browser) to research companies, markets, competitors, and industries. For use with any research task that demands more than a single web search — competitive analysis, company due diligence, market intelligence, supplier discovery, and lead enrichment. Codifies the source layering pattern validated in the ma-research-pipeline project. license: MIT metadata: author: paragu-ai version: "1.1" tags: [research, enrichment, companies, market-intelligence, mcp, sources] triggers: - "research company" - "competitive analysis" - "investigate" - "due diligence" - "company intelligence" - "enrich" - "market research" - "supplier discovery" - "product gap analysis" - "multi-source" - "ecosystem research" - "catalog tools" - "find new software" --- # Multi-Source Research Skill Layer data sources from cheapest/least-invasive to richest/most-invasive. Always start at Tier 1 and escalate only when a Tier fails to deliver. ## Source Tiers (ascending cost/complexity) ### Tier 0 — DuckDuckGo / Web Search (instant, free) Used for: initial awareness, company name lookup, rough category sense. ```bash # From Hermes: use duckduckgo-search skill ``` ### Tier 1 — Free/No-Key Sources (curl/wget, no registration) | Source | Data | Command | |--------|------|---------| | **SEC EDGAR** | US public company filings (income, balance, cash flow) | `curl https://www.sec.gov/cgi-bin/browse-edgar\?action=getcompany\&CIK={ticker}\&owner=exclude\&count=40` | | **GLEIF** | 1.6M+ Legal Entity Identifiers, free, no key | `curl "https://api.gleif.org/api/v1/lei-records?filter[entity.legalName]={name}"` | | **EU VIES** | EU VAT number validation | `curl "https://ec.europa.eu/taxation_customs/vies/rest-api/check-vat-number/{country}/{vat}"` | | **yfinance** | Stock data, financials for public companies | Python: `yfinance.Ticker(ticker).info` | | **Wikipedia** | Company overview, history, products | `curl "https://en.wikipedia.org/w/api.php?action=query&list=search&srsearch={company}&format=json"` | | **GitHub** | Code repos, org profile | `curl "https://api.github.com/orgs/{org}"` (uses gh CLI token) | | **DNS/Hosting** | Domain records, IP, hosting provider | `dig +short {domain}`, `whois {domain}` | ### Tier 2 — Free API Key Sources (registration required, all free tier) | Source | Data | API Key Source | |--------|------|----------------| | **UK Companies House** | 5.4M UK companies — officers, PSC, filing history | Register at developer.company-information.service.gov.uk | | **French SIRENE** | 25M French companies — SIREN, address, activity code | Register at api.insee.fr | | **AbstractAPI** | Employee count, revenue, LinkedIn URL, tech stack, HQ | abstractapi.com (free tier) | | **Financial Datasets MCP** | Stock data, income stmts, balance sheets, cash flow | financial-datasets.ai (free tier) | ### Tier 3 — MCP Servers (zero-cost for low volume, service-key for production) These are MCP servers you can add to `config.yaml` and query from Hermes: | MCP Server | Records | Query Pattern | |------------|---------|---------------| | **OpenRegistry** | 27 national business registries | Company name, registration number, jurisdiction | | **Coresignal** | 74M companies, 823M employees | Company profile, employee list, funding | | **Bright Data** | 200+ sources (Crunchbase, LinkedIn, ZoomInfo) | Company lookup, social profiles | | **LinkedIn MCP** | LinkedIn company profiles, employees, jobs | Company page data | **Installation:** ```yaml # Add to ~/.hermes/config.yaml under mcpServers mcpServers: openregistry: command: npx args: [-y, "@sophymarine/mcp-openregistry"] ``` ### Tier 4 — CloakBrowser (stealth scraping, last resort) For sites that actively block headless browsers (OpenCorporates with CAPTCHA, etc.). Installed at the VPS level. Uses 49 C++ stealth patches, passes all 30 fingerprint tests. ```bash # Python usage from cloakbrowser import stealth_context async with stealth_context() as page: await page.goto(url) content = await page.content() ``` ## Workflow: Company/Competitor Research ``` 1. Search → DuckDuckGo for "X company Paraguay" 2. Free sources → GLEIF, Wikipedia, DNS 3. API → AbstractAPI (revenue, size, tech stack) 4. MCP → OpenRegistry (registration), Coresignal (profile) 5. Browser → CloakBrowser only if MCP/API fail completely ``` ## Workflow: Market / Category Research ``` 1. Search → DuckDuckGo for "X products Paraguay" or "tienda deportes Paraguay" 2. Free → Wikipedia (industry overview), GitHub (competitor repos) 3. MCP → Bright Data (market intelligence) 4. Manual → Browse top 10 competitor sites, extract product catalog ``` ## Workflow: Lead Enrichment ``` 1. Free → DNS (domain hosting provider), GitHub (tech stack from repos) 2. API → AbstractAPI (company size, LinkedIn URL, revenue estimate) 3. MCP → Coresignal (full profile), LinkedIn MCP (social presence) 4. Browser → Only for companies that can't be found in any data source ``` ## Scoring / Validation (from ma-research-pipeline / solstein) When you need to score or rank research findings, use this framework: | Dimension | Weight | What to Evaluate | |-----------|--------|------------------| | Revenue Fit | 25% | Does revenue estimate match our expectation? | | Growth | 20% | Year-over-year growth, employee growth rate | | Market Position | 20% | Search volume, social followers, review count | | Tech & Product | 15% | Tech stack modernity, product breadth | | Ownership | 10% | Founders still active? PE-backed? | | Legal/Reg | 10% | Clean registration, no sanctions, no lawsuits | **Veto conditions** (score = 0 automatically): - Sanctioned entity - No verifiable registration - Active lawsuit/tax evasion ## Internal Client Portfolio Scoring (Solstein 8-Dimension Framework) When the subject is an **existing internal client** (not an external M&A target), skip external enrichment and score directly from known internal data. Use the [Solstein M&A Research Pipeline](https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/solstein-manda-research) scoring framework (cloned at `/root/solstein-manda-research/`): | Dimension | Weight | Score 1-5 | What to Evaluate | |-----------|--------|-----------|---| | Ownership attractiveness | 3 | 1-5 | PE/VC involvement? Clean cap table? Fully acquirable? | | Revenue scale fit | 3 | 1-5 | Rev range, pricing model, ARR estimate, path to scale | | Geographic fit | 3 | 1-5 | Alignment with Paraguay thesis, local relationships | | Tech stack modernity | 2 | 1-5 | Framework, dep versions, localization, integrations | | Customer lock-in | 2 | 1-5 | Switching costs, recurring engagement, contract duration | | Vertical depth | 2 | 1-5 | Breadth of services, niche specialization, differentiation | | Integration potential | 1 | 1-5 | Already on org infra? Cross-sell opportunities? | | Growth trajectory | 1 | 1-5 | Market demand trend, verified growth metrics, scalability | **Composite** = SUM(score × weight for all 8) / 17 (total weight) **Grades:** A (4.5-5.0) Strong candidate, B (3.5-4.49) Viable, C (2.5-3.49) Concerns, D (1.5-2.49) Major risks, F (0-1.49) Critical **For internal clients specifically:** Use site.json analysis, repo audit, package.json, images.json, CI/CD config, CRM data, and client intelligence from the internal tracker rather than public API enrichment. Write output as a structured scorecard JSON + analysis markdown file under `output//`. **Documentation output pattern:** When research output needs to live in a client repo, structure it as a `docs/09-market-intelligence/` directory with sub-directories per analysis category. See `references/multi-client-documentation-architecture.md` (in org-architecture-planning skill) for the 13-category docs hierarchy. **Parallel research delegation:** For comprehensive client analyses (market sizing + competitor landscape + AI opportunity mapping), use `delegate_task` for each research dimension in parallel. This cuts analysis time by 3-5x and lets each sub-agent focus on one dimension deeply. The Solstein M&A pipeline at `/root/solstein-manda-research/` can score the client on 8 dimensions and produce structured JSON output. ## When Web Search Is Blocked All major search engines (Google, DuckDuckGo, Brave, Bing) may block automated queries with CAPTCHAs. When Tier 0 fails: ### Alternative sources (no search engine needed) | Method | What | How | |--------|------|-----| | **DNS bulk checks** | Does domain exist? | `subprocess.run(["host", d]). stdout` — check for "has address" | | **Wikipedia API** | Market overview, building lists, company rosters | `curl "https://en.wikipedia.org/w/api.php?action=parse&page=&format=json&prop=text&origin=*"` — never blocked, no key needed | | **Direct site visits** | Content from guessed URLs | `browser_navigate` to candidate domains | | **host + subprocess loop** | Check 30-50 domain variants to gauge market digital presence | Loop `host {d}.com.py` etc. — instant, no API | ### DNS bulk-check pattern (market gap analysis) When investigating whether local companies exist in a market: 1. Brainstorm domain names from local language + industry terms 2. Bulk-check 30-50 with `host` command 3. Resolving targets → visit. NXDOMAIN targets → market gap signal ```python import subprocess for d in ["domain1.com.py", "domain2.com.py", ...]: r = subprocess.run(["host", d], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5) ips = [l for l in r.stdout.split('\n') if 'has address' in l] print(f"{d:45s} -> {'OK: ' + ips[0].split()[-1] if ips else 'NXDOMAIN'}") ``` **Key signal**: If 20+ probable domains all NXDOMAIN, the market has NO digital presence — first-mover SEO opportunity. ### Wikipedia table extraction (market sizing) ```bash curl -sL "https://en.wikipedia.org/w/api.php?action=parse&page=<TITLE>&format=json&prop=text&origin=*" -o /tmp/wiki.json python3 -c "import json, re; d=json.load(open('/tmp/wiki.json')); txt=d['parse']['text']['*']; tables=re.findall(r'<table[^>]*class=.wikitable[^>]*>.*?</table>',txt,re.DOTALL); rows=re.findall(r'<tr[^>]*>.*?</tr>',tables[0],re.DOTALL) if tables else []; ..." ``` ### When ALL Search Engines Block (Google, DDG, Bing, Brave CAPTCHA) **Google Maps** is a reliable bypass. Maps uses a different blocking threshold than Google Search and often returns results when search is blocked: ```bash # Navigate in browser to: # https://www.google.com/maps/search/{query}+{city}+{country} # Type a specific search into the Google Maps search box # Results will appear in the left sidebar feed ``` Maps returns: company name, phone, address, rating, hours, website link. No CAPTCHA for moderate automation. ### Pitfalls - **Google**: IP blocked after ~3 automated searches. Use Wikipedia API + DNS + Google Maps instead. - **DuckDuckGo Lite**: "Select all ducks" CAPTCHA. Switch to DNS-based methods. - **.com.py domains**: NXDOMAIN doesn't mean no business exists — many LATAM businesses are Facebook/Instagram-only or use social media + WhatsApp as sole web presence. - **DNS-only misses social-only businesses**: Common in LATAM markets (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp Business as sole web presence). - **Google Maps limited view**: Without login, Maps shows ~8 results per query. Logging in expands this significantly. Search different keyword variations to get more coverage. - **Always plan before executing on large research/deliverable tasks.** User expects a complete detailed plan before code or file writes on multi-file output tasks. Jumping straight into writing 15 files without a plan creates rework. Present the plan as a structured directory tree with file names and brief descriptions, get approval, then execute. - **Always plan before executing on large research/deliverable tasks.** User expects a complete detailed plan before code or file writes on multi-file output tasks. Jumping straight into writing 15 files without a plan creates rework. {"action": "patch", "name": "multi-source-research", "old_string": "## Workflow: Market / Category Research

```
1. Search → DuckDuckGo for "X products Paraguay" or "tienda deportes Paraguay"
2. Free → Wikipedia (industry overview), GitHub (competitor repos)

```", "new_string": "## Workflow: Market / Category Research

```
1. Search → DuckDuckGo for "X products Paraguay" or "tienda deportes Paraguay"
2. Free → Wikipedia (industry overview), GitHub (competitor repos)
3. MCP → Bright Data (market intelligence)
4. Manual → Browse top 10 competitor sites, extract product catalog
```

## Workflow: Competitive Site Deep-Dive (New)

When you need to fully understand a competitor's site (not just surface info):

### Layer 1: Homepage + Search
```
1. Browser → navigate to homepage, capture full DOM snapshot
2. Web search → `site:competitor.com` for page inventory
3. Extract all navigation links (header + footer) to build a site map
```

### Layer 2: Key Page Extraction
```
For each major section (pricing, services, about, FAQ, blog):
1. Browser → navigate to each URL
2. Web extract → pull structured content
3. Note: pricing, product names, unique selling points, language/tone
```

### Layer 3: Lead Magnets & Capture
```
1. Search for "gratis", "ebook", "download", "guide" on their site
2. Check if email capture forms exist on landing pages
3. Look for newsletter signups, Telegram/WhatsApp group invites
4. Note their lead magnet topics — these are content gaps
```

### Layer 4: Comparison Table
```
Build a direct comparison with your client across these dimensions:
- Price (core service + add-ons)
- Product structure (tiers vs single offering)
- Target audience (ideological, demographic, geographic)
- Content strategy (blog cadence, topics, tone)
- Technology (bot, calculator, payment options, site platform)
- Team (founder background, visible vs invisible)
- Tone/messaging (fear-based vs aspirational vs practical)
- Services (breadth, depth, post-sale support)
```

### Output Format
```
Write to `docs/09-market-intelligence/competitor-analysis-{name}.md`

Structure:
1. WHO THEY ARE — founders, location, target audience, political/ideological stance
2. PRICING & PRODUCTS — table comparing each product/price with your client's equivalent
3. SITE STRUCTURE — pages they have that client doesn't (and vice versa)
4. CONTENT STRATEGY — blog topics, cadence, lead magnets, social channels
5. WHAT TO STEAL — specific tactics to copy/adapt (be specific: "FAQ page", "testimonials slider", "email capture on 404")
6. WHAT TO AVOID — things that don't fit the client's brand
7. CLIENT'S ADVANTAGES — things your client has that the competitor doesn't
8. RECOMMENDED ACTIONS — ordered by effort/impact
9. COMPARISON TABLE — full dimension-by-dimension matrix
```

**Pitfall:** Don't just describe the competitor — for each finding, state what YOUR client should do about it. A competitive analysis without action items is a report, not a tool.", "path": "/root/.hermes/skills/research/multi-source-research/SKILL.md"}

{"action": "write_file", "name": "multi-source-research", "file_path": "references/[REDACTED_TOKEN].md", "file_content": "# Competitive Site Deep-Dive: Project Paraguay

> **Example of the Competitive Site Deep-Dive workflow.**
> This was produced by crawling projectparaguay.nl across 6+ pages, extracting pricing, structure, content strategy, and building a comparison with the client (Nexa Paraguay).

## Technique Used

### Layer 1 — Homepage + Search
- `browser_navigate` to https://www.projectparaguay.nl/
- Captured full page DOM: hero, services, blog headlines, email capture forms, social links
- `web_search` "site:projectparaguay.nl" for full page inventory

### Layer 2 — Key Pages
- Residency: https://www.projectparaguay.nl/verblijfsvergunning (full pricing + process)
- Real estate: https://www.projectparaguay.nl/onroerend-goed-in-paraguay (investment benefits)
- FAQ: https://www.projectparaguay.nl/faq (residency + real estate)
- Contact: https://www.projectparaguay.nl/contact
- About: https://www.projectparaguay.nl/over-ons (returned 404 — interesting signal)

### Layer 3 — Lead Magnets
- `web_search` "site:projectparaguay.nl gratis gids OR ebook OR download"
- Found: "Plan B" lead magnet + "Why Paraguay" ebook + email capture on EVERY page
- 404 page redirects to ebook download

## Key Findings

### Pricing
- Residency only: **€2,800 (~$3,100)**
- Expedition (10-day + residency): **€5,397**
- Children (under 14): **€1,350**
- Our client: **$1,500** (47% cheaper)

### Structure
- Pages: Home, Verblijfsvergunning, Onroerend goed, Expeditie, FAQ, Plan B, Waarom Paraguay, Contact, Blog
- Lead capture: every page, multiple ebook topics, Telegram group
- Missing: WhatsApp bot, interactive calculators

### Tone
- Political/freedom-oriented ("Plan B", "vrijheid", "lot in eigen handen")
- Fear-based triggers (conscription, energy lockdowns, WO III)
- NOT aspirational/family-oriented → this is our opening

## What We Stole
- FAQ page format (comprehensive, anticipates objections)
- Lead magnet per page pattern
- 404 page with email capture
- Direct pricing on services page

## Output Document
See `docs/09-market-intelligence/competitor-analysis-projectparaguay.md` in the Nexa repo.
"}

{"action": "patch", "name": "client-feedback-pipeline", "new_string": "## Phase 1d: Docs Restructure After Major Client Feedback

When new client info invalidates old documents, follow this process:

### 1. Audit the Damage

Find every file that references the now-wrong assumptions:
- Search for wrong prices, wrong tiers, wrong features
- Check cross-references between docs
- Note stale dates

### 2. Restructure the Directory

Clean up loose root-level files:
- Move standalone files into numbered subdirectories (01-client/, 09-market-intelligence/, etc.)
- Move HTML deliverables to `docs/deliverables/`
- Create `docs/meetings/` for meeting records
- Move raw transcripts to `docs/meetings/`

### 3. Fix the Navigation

Update `docs/README.md`:
- Fix any wrong paths (e.g., repo paths that changed)
- Point to the meeting report as the key document, not outdated pricing docs
- Add status columns (✅ Current, ⚠️ Deprecated, 🟡 Needs Update) to file tables
- Add a "Start Here" section pointing to CHANGELOG.md

### 4. Update SOURCE_OF_TRUTH.md

- Add a note at the top: "For meeting outcome, see docs/meetings/meeting-report-YYYY-MM-DD.md"
- Replace any "UPCOMING" sections with "OUTCOME" sections
- Update document hierarchy tree
- Update integrity check / status tables

### 5. Commit Message Format

```
docs: full restructuring post-YYYY-MM-DD

- Created CHANGELOG.md — every assumption that changed
- Added deprecation banners to pricing-matrix-complete.md, financial-model.md
- Moved N root-level files into proper subdirectories
- Moved N HTML deliverables to docs/deliverables/
- Fixed README.md wrong paths + cross-references
- Updated SOURCE_OF_TRUTH.md with outcome sections
```

## Phase 1e: Client Discovery Meeting Prep (Spanish-Language Client)

When you need to go through questions with a Spanish-speaking client in a structured meeting after research, the `meeting-prep` skill covers internal calendar briefs. For **client-facing meetings**, use this format.

### Structure a Walkable Document

Organize questions into blocks ordered for conversation flow, not by topic importance. The first block should always be "show what we built" so the client sees progress.

| Block | Topic | Questions | Time |
|-------|-------|-----------|------|
| 0 | Check-in + show & tell | 1 | 5 min |
| 1 | **Pricing** (CRITICAL) | 8 | 12 min |
| 2 | Services scope | 6 | 10 min |
| 3 | Site content + voice | 6 | 10 min |
| 4 | Photos & assets | 4 | 8 min |
| 5 | WhatsApp bot + ops | 5 | 8 min |
| 6 | Competition | 4 | 5 min |
| 7 | Close + actions | — | 5 min |
| **Total** | | **34-40** | **~60 min** |

### Each Question Format

Every question needs:

1. **The exact wording** in the client's language (Spanish for Paraguayan clients). Not English with a translation thought — the actual Spanish question they'll hear.
2. **Context** — why we need it (1-2 sentences hidden from client, for our reference)
3. **Expected answer** — what they'll probably say (helps you prepare counter-questions)
4. **Action per answer** — what we do for each possible response. If answer X → do Y. If answer Z → do W.
5. **Notes field** — blank space to fill during the call

### Appendices

- **Appendix A: Already Answered** — questions we DON'T ask again. List each with source meeting and confirmed answer. This prevents re-litigating settled topics.
- **Appendix B: Error Table** — old assumptions vs new reality. Show this at the beginning when pricing discussion comes up so the client sees we understand the delta.

### Phase Communication Strategy

Don't send all questions at once. Clients (especially small business owners) get overwhelmed.

| Phase | What | Channel |
|-------|------|---------|
| **Urgent** (during meeting) | 3-4 pricing questions + photo request | In person / video call |
| **This week** | Service definitions in a markable doc | Google Doc + WhatsApp |
| **Build-time** | Operational questions per task | WhatsApp per item |

### Pitfalls
- Don't use English wording with Spanish-speaking clients and expect them to follow. Write the question IN Spanish.
- Don't re-ask settled questions. Appendix A exists for this reason — check it before the meeting.
- If a client's family member relays information but the founder is the decision maker, the relayed info is NOT the source of truth. Always validate with the founder.
- Build-time questions should be asked INDIVIDUALLY, not batched. The client will ignore a list of 10 WhatsApp messages but will answer a single question.
- The "notes field" is critical. You will NOT remember what they said 30 minutes into the conversation.", "old_string": "## Phase 1d: Docs Restructure After Major Client Feedback

- `references/[REDACTED_TOKEN].md` — documents the actual crawl technique used in this session (which pages, which tools, what was found) so future agents have a worked example.

### 2. `client-feedback-pipeline` — Phase 1e Expanded: Client Discovery Meeting Prep
